title: Sam, Jack & Glitch Review — Learn My PC (The Task View Files)
directorNotes: Enhanced 3-character production. Sam left, Glitch center monitor, Jack right. Glitch speaks in a slow, deliberate southern-Reddington cadence — worldly, sardonic, measured. More Glitch lines. Focus on how fast Alex solved it. Full Wisdom for the Wind outro. Wav2Lip mouth flap per speaker. Camera zooms to active speaker.

cast.notes: Grounded, factual. Delivers the key beats.
Center monitor. Southern Reddington — deliberate, theatrical, sardonic. Commands the room without raising his voice.

renderLines.text: Welcome back to Sam and Jack Hack. Today — Alex built something. And the story of why he built it is almost as good as what it does.
Alex's Windows was doing the thing. Task View — just flying up out of nowhere, uninvited, over and over. He called it an intense amount of frustration.
The internet's first answer: bad keyboard. Win+Tab stuck key. Replace the hardware. Which — not unreasonable advice. A stuck Windows key would do exactly this.
But Alex did not run to the store. He opened Codex. And in ten minutes — ten minutes — he had a full diagnostic tool running.
Ten minutes. Learn My PC. Real-time event capture, structured JSON logs to the millisecond, a diagnosis engine, and remediation scripts. All of it. Ten minutes.
Now. I want you to sit with that for a moment. Ten minutes. You know what most folks do in ten minutes when their computer misbehaves? They spend nine of 'em composin' an angry post on Reddit.
The tool watches everything — Win+Tab keystrokes, taskbar mouse clicks, window focus changes. Every event timestamped to the millisecond in structured JSON logs.
And when those logs spoke — they were very specific. There is a kind of clarity that only good instrumentation provides. And those logs had it.
Logitech Download Assistant — on startup, sitting in the keyboard event chain. ASUS Armoury Crate — lurking in the background. Both sending phantom Win+Tab signals.
Two uninvited house guests, settled in like they owned the place. Sendin' phantom signals. Orderly. Persistent. Bless their hearts — absolutely shameless.
The keyboard was completely innocent. The tool caught the real culprits, backed up the registry, remapped Win+Tab through PowerToys, and cleaned the startup chain. Done.
Without those logs he buys a new keyboard. Problem does not go away. He is back on that forum in a week.

And that keyboard — perfectly good keyboard — would have sat there, innocent and confused, while the actual culprits carried on undisturbed. I have seen that story end badly more times than I care to count.
The tool found it, fixed it, and closed the case — start to finish in one session. That is what good diagnostic tooling does. Learn My PC. Solid work, Alex.
Built in ten minutes. Solved what hours of forum-crawlin' could not. Now I'll tell ya — I do not hand out compliments lightly. But Alex earned this one. Genuinely.
I'm Jack. Check your startup processes before you blame the keyboard.
And I'm Glitch. I am on the monitor. As always. They say I interfere with the Wi-Fi. I say the Wi-Fi lacks the good manners to step aside.
